g.data-narration: Welcome to this comprehensive lesson on database update operations, transactions, and handling constraint violations in DBMS.
Database update operations form the foundation of data manipulation, enabling applications to store and modify persistent data reliably.
g.text: • Every application needs to store, modify, and retrieve data
• Update operations (INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE) are essential SQL commands
• Transactions ensure data integrity in multi-user environments

g.data-narration: We will explore update operations, transaction management with ACID properties, constraint violations, and concurrency control mechanisms.
INSERT adds new rows to a table. Always ensure values match column data types and satisfy all constraints like NOT NULL and primary keys.
g.text: ✓ Match data types & ✓ Satisfy NOT NULL & ✓ Unique primary key

g.data-narration: UPDATE modifies existing rows. The WHERE clause is critical - it determines which rows get modified. Always verify with SELECT first.
g.text: UPDATE table_name SET col1=val1, col2=val2 WHERE condition;
⚠️ WARNING: Omitting WHERE updates ALL rows!

g.data-narration: DELETE removes rows from a table. Like UPDATE, the WHERE clause is essential. Without it, all rows are deleted but the table structure remains.
g.text: DELETE removes ROWS; table structure remains

g.data-narration: DELETE is DML with rollback capability, while TRUNCATE is DML but faster. TRUNCATE cannot be rolled back and resets identity columns.
A transaction is a logical unit of work containing one or more SQL operations. It ensures data integrity through the ACID properties.
ACID ensures reliable transactions: Atomicity is all-or-nothing, Consistency ensures valid states, Isolation makes concurrent execution appear serial, Durability saves changes permanently.
COMMIT permanently saves all changes in a transaction. ROLLBACK undoes all changes, restoring the database to its state before the transaction began.
g.text: UPDATE acc SET bal=bal-5000; UPDATE acc SET bal=bal+5000; COMMIT;

g.data-narration: SAVEPOINT creates intermediate points within a transaction, enabling partial rollback without aborting the entire transaction.
g.text: SAVEPOINT sp1; → ROLLBACK TO SAVEPOINT sp1;
Creates marker for partial undo without losing earlier changes

g.data-narration: Database constraints include PRIMARY KEY (unique non-null), FOREIGN KEY (referential integrity), UNIQUE (distinct values), CHECK (condition), and NOT NULL.
When constraints are violated, the DBMS rejects the operation. Foreign keys support ON DELETE CASCADE, SET NULL, RESTRICT, and NO ACTION for handling violations.
g.text: When constraints violated → DBMS REJECTS the operation
CASCADE | SET NULL | SET DEFAULT | RESTRICT | NO ACTION

g.data-narration: In concurrent environments, issues include lost updates (overwritten data), dirty reads (uncommitted data), non-repeatable reads (inconsistent results), and phantom reads (different row counts).

Four isolation levels control concurrency: READ UNCOMMITTED allows all problems, READ COMMITTED prevents dirty reads, REPEATABLE READ prevents non-repeatable reads, and SERIALIZABLE prevents all problems.
In summary: Use WHERE in UPDATE/DELETE carefully, TRUNCATE cannot rollback, ACID ensures reliability, and SERIALIZABLE prevents all concurrency issues.
g.text: 1. UPDATE/DELETE require WHERE clause - always verify with SELECT first
2. TRUNCATE is faster but cannot be rolled back (most databases)
3. COMMIT makes changes permanent; ROLLBACK undoes them
4. SAVEPOINT enables partial rollback within a transaction

g.data-narration: For exams: Remember ACID associations, DELETE vs TRUNCATE differences, constraint actions priority, and isolation level problem mappings.
